section.title: Lessons from Israel's History
section.sectionRationale: This long Asaphite maskil opens Book III's meditation on covenant history. Asaph turns Israel's wilderness story into a parable so the next generation will not repeat their fathers' forgetfulness, then traces God's history of patience through repeated rebellion to His sovereign choice of Judah, Zion, and David, the shepherd king.
section.thesis: Though Israel forgot God's wonders again and again and tested Him in the wilderness and in the land, God's compassion held back His full wrath, and His sovereign choice of David the shepherd king previews the greater Son of David who will shepherd His people forever.
section.frameNote: Eight units carry the history in order: the call to teach the next generation, 1-8; Ephraim's failure and God's wonders at the Exodus, 9-16; testing God for bread and meat, 17-31; shallow repentance and real compassion, 32-39; the plagues on Egypt, 40-51; led like a flock into the land, 52-55; rebellion in the land and the loss of Shiloh, 56-64; and God's choice of Judah, Zion, and David, 65-72.

verses.text: Give ear, O my people, to my instruction; listen to the words of my mouth.
I will open my mouth in {{mashal|parables}}; I will utter things hidden from the beginning,
that we have heard and known and our fathers have relayed to us.
We will not hide them from their children but will declare to the next generation the praises of the LORD and His might and the wonders He has performed.
For He established a testimony in Jacob and appointed a law in Israel, which He commanded our fathers to teach to their children,
that the coming generation would know them, even children yet to be born, to arise and tell their own children
that they should put their confidence in God, not forgetting His works, but keeping His commandments.
Then they will not be like their fathers, a stubborn and {{marah|rebellious}} generation, whose heart was not loyal, whose spirit was not faithful to God.
The archers of Ephraim turned back on the day of battle.
They failed to keep God's covenant and refused to live by His law.
They forgot what He had done, the wonders He had shown them.
He worked wonders before their fathers in the land of Egypt, in the region of Zoan.
He split the sea and brought them through; He set the waters upright like a wall.
He led them with a cloud by day and with a light of fire all night.
He split the rocks in the wilderness and gave them drink as abundant as the seas.
He brought streams from the stone and made water flow down like rivers.

units.label: Testing God for bread and meat

verses.text: But they continued to sin against Him, rebelling in the desert against the Most High.
They willfully {{nasah|tested}} God by demanding the food they craved.
They spoke against God, saying, "Can God really prepare a table in the wilderness?
When He struck the rock, water gushed out and torrents raged. But can He also give bread or supply His people with meat?"
Therefore the LORD heard and was filled with wrath; so a fire was kindled against Jacob, and His anger flared against Israel,
because they did not believe God or rely on His salvation.
Yet He commanded the clouds above and opened the doors of the heavens.
He rained down manna for them to eat; He gave them grain from heaven.
Man ate the bread of angels; He sent them food in abundance.
He stirred the east wind from the heavens and drove the south wind by His might.
He rained meat on them like dust, and winged birds like the sand of the sea.
He felled them in the midst of their camp, all around their dwellings.
So they ate and were well filled, for He gave them what they craved.
Yet before they had filled their desire, with the food still in their mouths,
God's anger flared against them, and He put to death their strongest and subdued the young men of Israel.

units.label: Shallow repentance, real compassion
units.note: God remembers they are but flesh

verses.text: In spite of all this, they kept on sinning; despite His wonderful works, they did not believe.
So He ended their days in futility, and their years in sudden terror.
When He slew them, they would seek Him; they repented and searched for God.
And they remembered that God was their Rock, that God Most High was their Redeemer.
But they deceived Him with their mouths, and lied to Him with their tongues.
Their hearts were disloyal to Him, and they were unfaithful to His covenant.
And yet He was compassionate; He forgave their iniquity and did not destroy them. He often restrained His anger and did not unleash His full wrath.
He remembered that they were but flesh, a passing breeze that does not return.
How often they disobeyed Him in the wilderness and grieved Him in the desert!
Again and again they tested God and provoked the Holy One of Israel.
They did not remember His power, the day He redeemed them from the adversary,
when He performed His signs in Egypt and His wonders in the fields of Zoan.
He turned their rivers to blood, and from their streams they could not drink.
He sent swarms of flies that devoured them, and frogs that devastated them.
He gave their crops to the grasshopper, the fruit of their labor to the locust.
He killed their vines with hailstones and their sycamore-figs with sleet.
He abandoned their cattle to the hail and their livestock to bolts of lightning.
He unleashed His fury against them, wrath, indignation, and calamity, a band of destroying angels.
He cleared a path for His anger; He did not spare them from death but delivered their lives to the plague.
He struck all the firstborn of Egypt, the virility in the tents of Ham.

units.label: Led like a flock into the land

verses.text: He led out His people like sheep and guided them like a flock in the wilderness.
He led them safely, so they did not fear, but the sea engulfed their enemies.
He brought them to His holy land, to the mountain His right hand had acquired.
He drove out nations before them and apportioned their inheritance; He settled the tribes of Israel in their tents.

units.label: Rebellion in the land, loss of Shiloh

verses.text: But they tested and disobeyed God Most High, for they did not keep His decrees.
They turned back and were faithless like their fathers, twisted like a faulty bow.
They enraged Him with their high places and provoked His jealousy with their idols.
On hearing it, God was furious and rejected Israel completely.
He abandoned the tabernacle of Shiloh, the tent He had pitched among men.
He delivered His strength to captivity, and His splendor to the hand of the adversary.
He surrendered His people to the sword because He was enraged by His heritage.
Fire consumed His young men, and their maidens were left without wedding songs.
His priests fell by the sword, but their widows could not lament.

units.label: God's choice of Judah, Zion, and David
units.note: shepherd king after God's own heart

verses.text: Then the Lord awoke as from sleep, like a mighty warrior overcome by wine.
He beat back His foes; He put them to everlasting shame.
He rejected the tent of Joseph and refused the tribe of Ephraim.
But He {{bachar|chose}} the tribe of Judah, Mount Zion, which He loved.
He built His sanctuary like the heights, like the earth He has established forever.
He chose David His servant and took him from the sheepfolds;
from tending the ewes He brought him to be {{raah|shepherd}} of His people Jacob, of Israel His inheritance.
So David shepherded them with integrity of heart and guided them with skillful hands.

mashal.gloss: A parable, proverb, or extended comparison teaching wisdom through story. Asaph will not merely list facts but shape Israel's history into a parable so it lands on the heart; Matthew 13:35 applies this very verse to Jesus' teaching in parables.

marah.gloss: To be rebellious, defiant, or bitter against authority. Naming the fathers 'a stubborn and rebellious generation' identifies the deep pattern beneath every failure that follows in the psalm.

nasah.gloss: To test or try. Israel tested God, demanding proof of His care even after seeing His wonders, turning the tables on who was really on trial in the wilderness.

bachar.gloss: To choose or elect. God rejected the tent of Joseph but chose the tribe of Judah, Mount Zion, and David, a sovereign, gracious election that runs forward to the greater Son of David.

raah.gloss: To shepherd, tend, or pasture. David is taken from tending ewes to shepherd God's people, the same verb used of God's own care for Israel like a flock, joining king and God in one image fulfilled in Christ, the Good Shepherd.

basic.context: Asaph turns Israel's whole wilderness history into a long parable so that the next generation will hope in God and not repeat their fathers' pattern. Again and again God works wonders, Israel forgets and rebels, judgment falls, and yet God's compassion holds back His full wrath. The psalm ends by tracing God's sovereign move away from failed Ephraim to His chosen king, David, a humble shepherd raised up to shepherd God's own flock.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real history told for a pastoral purpose, to instruct the generation being taught rather than merely to record facts. The refrain 'they forgot, yet He remembered' is the psalm's engine; each rebellion and each act of divine patience is meant to be heard and applied, not simply observed.
basic.originalLanguages: Asaph calls his song a mashal, a parable, the very word Matthew 13:35 quotes to explain why Jesus taught in parables. He names Israel's sin marah, rebellious, and their provocation nasah, testing God. At the end God bachar, chooses, Judah and David, whom He sets to raah, shepherd, His people, language that flows straight into Christ.
basic.typology: The manna, bread from heaven that still left Israel hungry and unbelieving, points to Jesus, the true bread of life, John 6:31-35. David, taken from the sheepfolds to shepherd Israel, previews Christ, the great Son of David and the Good Shepherd who will not fail as Israel's kings and generations did.
basic.christ: Matthew 13:34-35 cites Psalm 78:2 directly, 'I will open my mouth in parables,' as the reason Jesus taught in parables, showing the psalm itself pointing to Christ's teaching ministry. The psalm's climax, God choosing David the shepherd, anticipates Jesus, the Son of David who shepherds His people with perfect integrity, John 10:11.
basic.god: God is patient almost past belief, forgiving Israel's shallow, repeated unfaithfulness and remembering that they are but flesh, yet He is also just, letting real consequences fall on persistent rebellion, from wilderness plagues on the disobedient to the loss of Shiloh. His sovereign choice of Judah and David shows He is free to redirect history according to His own grace.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 13:34-35 quotes this psalm's opening as the reason Jesus taught in parables. John 6:31-35 shows Jesus as the true bread from heaven that the manna only foreshadowed. 1 Corinthians 10:1-11 uses this same wilderness history as a warning to the church. John 10:11 shows Jesus as the Good Shepherd David's kingship anticipated.
basic.oneStory: This psalm rehearses Israel's history precisely so hope in God will survive to the next generation. Its pattern of forgetting and mercy, judgment and patience, runs all the way to God's choice of David the shepherd, and from David the story runs on to Jesus, the greater Shepherd and Son of David who teaches in parables and never forgets His flock.

context.literary: A long maskil of Asaph structured as history turned parable, alternating cycles of divine wonder, human forgetfulness, judgment, and renewed mercy, before climaxing in God's election of Judah, Zion, and David.
context.historical: Likely composed reflecting on the loss and return of the Ark, 1 Samuel 4-7, and the shift of covenant leadership from Ephraim and Shiloh to Judah, Zion, and the house of David, giving the psalm both ancient memory and immediate relevance for a generation needing to learn from that history.
context.canonical: One of the Bible's great rehearsals of redemptive history, standing alongside Psalm 105, 106, and Nehemiah 9, and directly quoted by Jesus as the reason for His parables, Matthew 13:35.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To press Israel's history of rebellion and God's patience onto the next generation so they will set their hope in God, not forget His works, and keep His commandments, rather than repeating their fathers' failures.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes specific historical rebellions and specific acts of judgment and mercy; it prescribes as an abiding pattern that God's people must actively teach the next generation, must not presume on wonders already seen, and must trust God's compassion even after repeated failure.

debates.question: Does verse 65, 'the Lord awoke as from sleep,' imply a real change in God, or is it figurative language for His action?

views.label: A vivid anthropomorphism describing God's sudden, decisive intervention, without implying He was literally inactive or asleep
views.case: Scripture elsewhere insists God neither slumbers nor sleeps, Psalm 121:4, so the image describes how deliverance felt to Israel rather than a change in God's unceasing attention.

views.label: A genuine picture of God's dynamic engagement with history, entering fully into the drama of judgment and rescue
views.case: The psalm's whole narrative shape, wrath rising and mercy responding, takes God's real involvement in time seriously rather than reducing every action to timeless decree.

debates.bearing: Either reading upholds both God's unchanging character and the psalm's confidence that He genuinely acts within Israel's history to save.

study.originalLanguages: Asaph calls this history a mashal, a parable, the same word Matthew 13:35 quotes about Jesus' teaching. Israel's sin is marah, stubborn rebellion, and their provocation is nasah, testing God who had already proven Himself. At the climax God bachar, chooses, Judah and David, setting him to raah, shepherd, His people, language the New Testament applies directly to Christ.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the true bread that came down from heaven, who alone satisfies forever, John 6:31-35.

typology.type: David taken from the sheepfolds to shepherd Israel, verses 70-72
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the great Son of David and the Good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep, John 10:11.

typology.type: The psalm's own form as a mashal, a parable, verse 2
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' teaching in parables, explicitly linked to this verse in Matthew 13:34-35.

study.christ: Matthew 13:34-35 quotes Psalm 78:2 directly to explain why Jesus taught the crowds in parables, 'things hidden from the beginning' now unveiled in His teaching. The psalm's climax, God's rejection of Ephraim and choice of David, a shepherd raised from the sheepfolds to shepherd God's people with integrity of heart, verse 72, anticipates Jesus, the greater Son of David who is Himself the Good Shepherd, John 10:11, and the true bread from heaven the manna could only foreshadow, John 6:31-35.
study.god: God is astonishingly patient, remembering that His people are but flesh and restraining His full wrath through cycle after cycle of rebellion, yet He is also just, letting real judgment fall in the wilderness and at Shiloh when covenant loyalty collapsed into idolatry. His freedom to reject Ephraim and choose Judah and David shows His grace is sovereign, not owed to any tribe's status.

crossReferences.note: Quotes Psalm 78:2 as the reason Jesus taught the crowds in parables.
Jesus identifies Himself as the true bread from heaven that the wilderness manna foreshadowed.
Paul uses this same wilderness history as a direct warning to the New Testament church.
Jesus as the Good Shepherd fulfills the shepherd-king pattern set by David in this psalm.

study.oneStory: Psalm 78 rehearses Israel's history precisely so hope in God will survive to the next generation, tracing a pattern of forgetfulness met by patient mercy that finally issues in God's choice of David the shepherd. That pattern and that choice run forward to Jesus, the greater Shepherd and Son of David, who teaches in parables just as this psalm foretold and who never forgets His flock.

sermon.title: Educating for Hope
sermon.why: Piper preaches directly from Psalm 78:1-8 on why God's people must teach the next generation His works so that they set their hope in God, the exact pastoral burden driving this psalm.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's stated purpose in verses 1-7?

options.text: To recount God's wonders so the next generation will set their hope in Him and not repeat their fathers' rebellion.
options.note: Verses 6-7 state this purpose explicitly.

options.text: To entertain the congregation with stories about Israel's past.
options.note: The psalm's aim is pastoral instruction, not entertainment.

options.text: To list Israel's kings for genealogical record.
options.note: The psalm recounts events and responses, not a genealogy.

options.text: To excuse Israel's disobedience as unavoidable.
options.note: The psalm names Israel's sin honestly rather than excusing it, though it does explain the pattern.

quiz.q: Despite the manna and quail God provided, how did Israel respond, verses 17-32?

options.text: They continued to test God and did not believe, even with the food still in their mouths.
options.note: Verses 30-32 describe judgment falling while the food was still being eaten.

options.text: They gave thanks briefly and then were satisfied forever.
options.note: Any gratitude was short-lived; the psalm stresses their continued unbelief.

options.text: They refused to eat the food God provided.
options.note: They ate eagerly, verse 29; the sin was ingratitude and unbelief, not refusal.

options.text: They never sinned again after being fed.
options.note: Verse 32 says plainly that in spite of all this they kept on sinning.

quiz.q: How does the psalm describe God's response to Israel's shallow, repeated unfaithfulness, verses 36-39?

options.text: He was compassionate, forgave their iniquity, and remembered they were but flesh, restraining His full wrath.
options.note: Verses 38-39 state this directly, even after naming their deceit in verses 36-37.

options.text: He ignored their sin as though it did not matter.
options.note: The psalm names their sin honestly rather than ignoring it.

options.text: He immediately and permanently destroyed the whole nation.
options.note: Judgment did fall at points, but the psalm stresses restraint, not total destruction.

options.text: He waited for them to earn forgiveness through good works.
options.note: The emphasis falls on God's own compassion and memory of their frailty, not on Israel earning anything.

quiz.q: What happened to the tabernacle at Shiloh, and why, verses 58-64?

options.text: God abandoned it and gave His people to the sword because they provoked Him with idols and high places.
options.note: Verses 58-62 tie the loss of Shiloh directly to Israel's idolatry.

options.text: It was peacefully relocated to Jerusalem by royal decree.
options.note: The psalm describes judgment and loss, not an orderly, peaceful transfer.

options.text: It was destroyed by a natural disaster unrelated to sin.
options.note: The text explicitly ties the loss to covenant unfaithfulness, not to an unrelated disaster.

options.text: It was rebuilt immediately afterward in the same location.
options.note: The psalm records loss and God's later choice of Zion instead, not a rebuilding at Shiloh.

quiz.q: Whom did God choose instead of Ephraim, and what does this anticipate, verses 67-72?

options.text: Judah, Zion, and David the shepherd king, anticipating Christ, the Son of David and the Good Shepherd.
options.note: Verses 68-72 name this choice, which the New Testament applies to Jesus.

options.text: Benjamin, anticipating Saul's kingship.
options.note: The psalm names Judah and David, not Benjamin or Saul.

options.text: No one; Ephraim continued to lead Israel afterward.
options.note: The text explicitly says God rejected Ephraim and chose Judah instead.

options.text: The Philistines, who then ruled over Israel.
options.note: The Philistines appear earlier as adversaries, not as God's chosen leadership for His people.

questions: The psalm exists so 'the next generation' would set their hope in God, verse 7. What are you doing, practically, to pass on God's works to those who come after you?
Israel forgot what God had done for them again and again, verse 11. What has God done for you that you are tempted to forget when new trials come?
Even when Israel's hearts were 'not loyal' and 'not faithful,' God 'was compassionate' and restrained His anger, verses 37-38. Where do you need to receive that same patient compassion today?
God chose the humble shepherd David over the more prominent tribe of Ephraim, verses 67-70. Where might you be tempted to trust in status rather than in God's sovereign, gracious choice?
David shepherded Israel 'with integrity of heart and skillful hands,' verse 72, a picture fulfilled in Jesus. How does knowing Christ is your perfect Shepherd change how you follow Him this week?

translationNotes.note: The phrase 'open my mouth in parables' and 'things hidden from the beginning' is quoted directly in Matthew 13:35 to explain why Jesus taught in parables, giving this verse a direct New Testament fulfillment.
The reason for Ephraim's retreat 'on the day of battle' is left unstated here; the following verses supply it by describing a pattern of forgetting God's covenant.
'The Lord awoke as from sleep' is a bold, figurative picture (an anthropomorphism) for God's sudden, decisive action to save; it does not mean God was ever literally inactive or asleep.

meta.notes: BSB text for Psalm 78 fetched from biblehub.com/bsb/psalms/78.htm (Berean Standard Bible) via WebSearch and web_fetch, because the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error. Footnote reference markers removed; two em dashes in verse 6, one in verse 42 (with a footnote marker), and one in verse 49 were converted to commas. Verse count (72) and first/last verse wording checked against the section map. Sermon link verified this session: John Piper, 'Educating for Hope' (desiringgod.org), preached on Psalm 78:1-8.
